retrieved_docs.content: Impaired glucose tolerance or impaired fasting glucose Abnormal uric acid metabolism Dyslipidemia (increased triglycerides, decreased HDL-C, or small, dense LDL) Hemodynamic changes such as elevated blood pressure Prothrombotic factors (PAI-1, fibrinogen) Markers of inflammation (eg, C-reactive protein, white blood cell count) Endothelial dysfunction Other factors include the following: Body mass index (BMI) greater than or equal to 25 kg/m 2 Diagnosis of CVD, PCOS, NAFLD, or acanthosis nigricans A family history of T2D, hypertension, or CVD Sedentary lifestyle Non-white ethnicity Age older than 40 years

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Vascular and Lymphatic Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Arterial Sonography
retrieved_docs.content: Low resistance vessels classically show a monophasic waveform (see Image. Monophasic Waveform). [4] These waveforms are found in arteries supplying organs with high oxygen demand, such as the internal carotid, renal, umbilical, and arteries supplying the gut after a meal. In contradistinction to high resistance vessels, these vessels and their corresponding downstream vessels are continually dilated to allow for increased oxygenation. The gut requires increased blood flow during digestion and less blood flow during times of fasting. Vessels such as the superior mesenteric artery and the inferior mesenteric artery show low-resistance waveforms during digestion and high-resistance waveforms during fasting. [6] In a low-resistance waveform, there are typically two physiologic portions that may be visualized:

retrieved_docs.title: Doppler Extra-Cranial Carotid Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Spectral analysis provides critical parameters, including peak systolic velocity (PSV), peak diastolic velocity (PDV), mean maximum velocity, and pulsatility index. Spectral Doppler waveform evaluation offers detailed insights into flow dynamics at the sampling site, influenced by hemodynamic factors affecting proximal or distal segments of the vessel. [19] The ICA exhibits low-resistance flow, the ECA shows high-resistance flow, and the CCA demonstrates a hybrid pattern of both (see Images . Internal Carotid Artery on Doppler Ultrasonography, External Carotid Artery on Doppler Ultrasonography, and Common Carotid Artery on Doppler Ultrasonography). The resistive index describes waveforms, indicating resistance in the vessel distal to the examined segment. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Renovascular Hypertension -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Duplex ultrasonography can also measure the resistive index (RI), which is calculated as (PSV-End diastolic velocity)/PSV. A value of more than 0.7 indicates the presence of pathological resistance to flow, and studies have shown that a value >0.8 predicts poor response to revascularization treatments. [14] The most significant setbacks for duplex ultrasonography are its reduced sensitivity in obese patients, hindrance by overlying bowel gas, and operator dependence.

retrieved_docs.title: Orbital Color Doppler Imaging -- Technique or Treatment -- Doppler Indices
retrieved_docs.content: Resistivity index: The ratio of the difference between peak systolic velocity and end-diastolic velocity to the value of the peak systolic velocity during the same cycle of the Doppler waveform. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Arterial Doppler Imaging, ABI, and Plethysmography -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Continuous-wave Doppler
retrieved_docs.content: The result from continuous wave doppler is a sound, which corresponds to the velocity of blood flow within a vessel. A typical peripheral artery at rest will have a triphasic or biphasic quality of sound, which corresponds to forward flow in systole, a brief reversal of flow in early diastole, then forward flow in late diastole. With worsening arterial disease, flow is dampened, as is the resultant signal. In a diseased vessel, triphasic or biphasic signals give way to a monophasic pattern with a lower amplitude. Of note, in low resistance vessels, such as the carotid artery, there is no reversal of flow; the resulting signal is monophasic, corresponding to constant forward flow.

retrieved_docs.title: Comprehensive Assessment and Diagnosis of Metabolic and Biomechanical Complications in Obesity -- Function -- Pediatric obesity-specific considerations
retrieved_docs.content: A BMI at or above the 85th percentile demonstrates a significant association with hypertension when compared to nonobese children. The prevalence of prehypertension and hypertension varies notably by weight category: 7.1% in children with normal weight, 21.9% in those classified as overweight, and 42.3% in those with obesity. [5] The pathophysiology of obesity-related hypertension in pediatric populations involves multiple mechanisms, including activation of the sympathetic nervous system, the presence of insulin resistance, and vascular dysfunction, all of which contribute to elevated blood pressure and increased long-term cardiovascular risk. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Fetal Growth Restriction -- Evaluation -- Umbilical Artery Doppler Velocimetry
retrieved_docs.content: Trained technicians evaluate umbilical artery Doppler velocimetry by measuring the peak systolic velocity, the frequency shift, the end-diastolic frequency shift, and the mean peak frequency shift over the cardiac cycle according to various ratios, including the systolic to diastolic ratio, resistance index, and pulsatility index. Umbilical placental insufficiency is reflected in these ratios, which increase abnormally as gestational age progresses instead of decreasing, as seen with normal placental function. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Blood Pressure Age Related Changes -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Two different waves form the arterial pressure wave: a forward wave and a reflected wave. The forward wave represents blood flow from the left ventricle during contraction. The reflected or reverse wave is created by structures that cause impedance (e.g., branch points, direction changes). The speed of the wave is known as the pulse wave velocity. In a young artery, the reflected wave reflects to the aortic root during diastole. In an older, stiffened artery, the reflected wave reflects to the aortic root during systole because it has an increased pulse wave velocity, allowing it to contribute to the forward wave resulting in an increased peak forward wave. Depending on the device used, a pulse wave velocity greater than 10 to 12 m/sec indicates arterial wall stiffening. [6] Other test modalities for increased arterial pressure include but are not limited to regular blood pressure checks with a sphygmomanometer, ankle-brachial index, angiography, and stress testing.

Collectively, these relationships illustrate an integrated network in which the heart serves as the primary site for electrophysiologic monitoring, invasive diagnostics, mechanical circulatory support, rhythm‑management device therapy, and surgical interventions, while its anatomical constituents and associated diseases are systematically linked to procedural hierarchies and systemic risk factors, underscoring the importance of comprehensive cardiac knowledge for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2154
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses the percutaneous insertion of catheters into several major arteries for the purpose of hemodynamic assessment and continuous vascular surveillance. Specifically, this monitoring approach includes catheter placement in the left carotid artery, left femoral artery, left radial artery, right axillary artery, right carotid artery, and right temporal artery. By accessing these distinct arterial sites, clinicians can obtain precise pressure measurements, blood flow data, and other critical cardiovascular parameters, thereby enhancing diagnostic accuracy and guiding therapeutic interventions in patients with cardiovascular disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 953
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring is a comprehensive clinical practice that includes systemic arterial pressure monitoring as a subordinate component. It focuses on the percutaneous insertion of arterial catheters for continuous hemodynamic assessment, with specific techniques described for the left and right axillary arteries, left and right femoral arteries, left and right radial arteries, left and right temporal arteries, and the right carotid artery. Each of these catheter‑based approaches is employed to obtain accurate arterial pressure data and to support real‑time cardiovascular evaluation. In addition to arterial catheterization, cardiovascular monitoring is classified as a form of transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring and is also considered a type of hemodynamic monitoring (hemodynamické monitorování). Together, these modalities provide clinicians with detailed information on systemic arterial pressures and overall cardiac function, facilitating precise diagnosis, therapeutic decision‑making, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 903
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a broad range of diagnostic and invasive techniques aimed at assessing cardiac function and systemic hemodynamics. It includes electrocardiogram monitoring such as the “Electrocardiogram monitoring, 10 hour portrait” and specialized fetal electrocardiogram, as well as comprehensive monitoring of ECG, arterial pressure, blood gases, and cardiac output. Invasive arterial monitoring is achieved through percutaneous insertion of catheters into various vessels, including the left carotid artery, left temporal artery, right axillary artery, right carotid artery, right femoral artery, and right temporal artery, each providing direct pressure and flow data from the respective arterial sites. Cardiovascular monitoring also incorporates transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring, which offers real‑time imaging of cardiac structures via the esophagus, and hemodynamické monitorování (hemodynamic monitoring), which evaluates overall circulatory status. Together, these modalities provide clinicians with detailed information for the diagnosis, management, and therapeutic guidance of patients with cardiovascular and systemic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2870
retrieved_docs.content: Atherosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي) is clinically associated with several factors and signs, including tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ), routine health maintenance (حفظ الصحة الروتيني), the presence of a limp (عرج), carotid artery bruit (لغط الشريان السباتي), and femoral artery bruit (لغط فخذي). In addition, atherosclerosis has a specific subtype referred to as generalized atherosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي المعمم).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2299
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis is mapped to تكلس أبهري, indicating that the Arabic term تكلس أبهري refers to the atherosclerotic changes occurring in peripheral and visceral arterial vessels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2695
retrieved_docs.content: Arteriosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي) is clinically associated with renal dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى), a systolic murmur (النفخة الانقباضية), tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ), routine health maintenance (حفظ الصحة الروتيني), and the presence of a limp (عرج). Conversely, renal dysfunction is also clinically associated with arteriosclerosis, highlighting a bidirectional relationship between vascular stiffening and kidney impairment. The systolic murmur may reflect hemodynamic alterations caused by stiffened arterial walls, while tobacco use is a well‑known risk factor that accelerates the development and progression of arteriosclerotic changes. Routine health maintenance practices are important for early detection and management of these interrelated conditions. A limp can arise from peripheral arterial disease secondary to arteriosclerosis, underscoring the systemic impact of arterial pathology. Recognizing and addressing these interconnected associations is essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2895
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure is classified as a vascular condition, and it belongs to the broader Vascular category that also encompasses أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis), فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (diastolic hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension). These entities represent related subtypes of abnormal blood pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a persistently higher average arterial pressure, while hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation that can threaten organ function. Diastolic hypertension is characterized by consistently high diastolic pressure, and systolic hypertension is defined by consistently high systolic pressure. Recognizing that all of these disorders fall under the Vascular umbrella aids clinicians in categorizing blood‑pressure abnormalities, tailoring diagnostic evaluation, and selecting appropriate therapeutic strategies to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1936
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure, hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), and reactive hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) are all classified within the broader vascular category. These conditions represent distinct manifestations of abnormal vascular pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a sustained increase in the average arterial pressure throughout the cardiac cycle. Hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that can threaten organ function if not promptly treated. Primary pulmonary hypertension is a specific form of high pressure that originates in the pulmonary arteries, leading to right‑ventricular strain and potential respiratory complications. Reactive hypertension describes a secondary rise in systemic blood pressure that occurs in response to another underlying condition. Recognizing that these entities share a common vascular basis aids clinicians in assessing, differentiating, and managing patients with hypertension‑related disorders, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 921
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is identified and interpreted through increased blood pressure measurements, which are central to its clinical assessment. Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis exhibits clinical features that are similar to those of essential hypertension, highlighting overlapping manifestations and the need to consider both conditions when evaluating patients with elevated blood pressure and related renal changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1496
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي (arteriosclerosis) is clinically associated with several findings and risk factors, including النفخة الانقباضية (a systolic murmur), تعاطي التَبغ (tobacco use), حفظ الصحة الروتيني (routine health maintenance), عرج (limp or gait disturbance), and لغط الشريان السباتي (carotid bruit). In addition, التصلب العصيدي has a recognized subtype called التصلب العصيدي المعمم (generalized arteriosclerosis), reflecting a more extensive form of the disease. Recognizing these associations aids clinicians in identifying arteriosclerotic disease, assessing related vascular signs, and emphasizing preventive measures such as routine health maintenance and smoking cessation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1753
retrieved_docs.content: Arteriosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي) is clinically associated with renal dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى), a systolic murmur (النفخة الانقباضية), routine health maintenance (حفظ الصحة الروتيني), a limp (عرج), and a carotid bruit (لغط الشريان السباتي). Generalized arteriosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي المعمم) is a specific subtype that falls under the broader category of arteriosclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1112
retrieved_docs.content: Arteriosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي) is clinically associated with several manifestations and conditions. It is linked to renal dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى), indicating that impaired kidney function often co‑occurs with arterial stiffening. Patients with arteriosclerosis may present with a systolic murmur (النفخة الانقباضية) and a carotid bruit (لغط الشريان السباتي), reflecting turbulent blood flow through narrowed vessels. The disease can also produce a limp (عرج) due to reduced perfusion of the lower extremities, and routine health maintenance (حفظ الصحة الروتيني) is emphasized as an important strategy for monitoring and managing the condition. Arteriosclerosis is classified as a subtype of chronic unspecified ischemic heart disease (مرض قلبي إقفاري مزمن، غير محدد), situating it within the broader spectrum of ischemic cardiac disorders. It has a more specific descendant, generalized arteriosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي المعمم), which represents a widespread form of the disease. Additionally, vascular ischemia is recognized as a related pathological process, underscoring the role of arteriosclerotic vessel narrowing in reducing tissue blood flow. Together, these relationships highlight the multifaceted clinical picture of arteriosclerosis and its relevance to cardiovascular, renal, and peripheral health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 181
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is recognized as a finding in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) and is classified under the broader condition of peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي), which serves as a parent concept for the “Blood, Heart and Circulation” category and the diagnosis chronic unspecified ischemic heart disease (مرض قلبي إقفاري مزمن، غير محدد). This atherosclerotic disease, also termed cardiovascular atherosclerotic disease (داء تصلب الشرايين القلبي الوعائي), is linked to vascular ischemia, increased risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, and is associated with a range of clinical manifestations including chest constriction (خُنَاقٌ صَدْرِيّ), a bronchial finding (ذَبْحَةُ برنزميتال), systolic murmur (النفخة الانقباضية), routine health maintenance (حفظ الصحة الروتيني), limp or gait abnormality (عرج), carotid bruit (لغط الشريان السباتي), femoral bruit (لغط فخذي), and abnormal electrocardiogram (مخطط كهربية القلب غير طبيعي).
